openalex.title: Characterizing the ELG luminosity functions in the nearby Universe
openalex.display_name: Characterizing the ELG luminosity functions in the nearby Universe

locations.raw_source_name: Favole, G, Gonzalez-Perez, V, Ascasibar, Y, Corcho-Caballero, P, Montero-Dorta, A D, Benson, A J, Comparat, J, Cora, S A, Croton, D, Guo, H, Izquierdo-Villalba, D, Knebe, A, Orsi, Stoppacher, D & Vega-Martínez, C A 2024, 'Characterizing the ELG luminosity functions in the nearby Universe', Astronomy and Astrophysics, vol. 683, A46. https://doi.org/10.1051/0004-6361/202346443

Favole, G, Gonzalez-Perez, V, Ascasibar, Y, Corcho-Caballero, P, Montero-Dorta, A D, Benson, A J, Comparat, J, Cora, S A, Croton, D, Guo, H, Izquierdo-Villalba, D, Knebe, A, Orsi, Stoppacher, D & Vega-Martínez, C A 2024, 'Characterizing the ELG luminosity functions in the nearby Universe', Astronomy and Astrophysics, vol. 683, A46. https://doi.org/10.1051/0004-6361/202346443

crossref.abstract:
<jats:p><jats:italic>Context.</jats:italic> Nebular emission lines are powerful diagnostics for the physical processes at play in galaxy formation and evolution. Moreover, emission-line galaxies (ELGs) are one of the main targets of current and forthcoming spectroscopic cosmological surveys.</jats:p>
<jats:p><jats:italic>Aims.</jats:italic> We investigate the contributions to the line luminosity functions (LFs) of different galaxy populations in the local Universe, providing a benchmark for future surveys of earlier cosmic epochs.</jats:p>
<jats:p><jats:italic>Methods.</jats:italic> The large statistics of the observations from the SDSS DR7 main galaxy sample and the MPA-JHU spectral catalog enabled us to precisely measure the H<jats:italic>α</jats:italic>, H<jats:italic>β</jats:italic>, [O <jats:sc>II</jats:sc>], [O <jats:sc>III</jats:sc>], and, for the first time, the [N <jats:sc>II</jats:sc>], and [S <jats:sc>II</jats:sc>] emission-line LFs over ∼2.4 Gyrs in the low-<jats:italic>z</jats:italic> Universe, 0.02 < <jats:italic>z</jats:italic> < 0.22. We present a generalized 1/<jats:italic>V</jats:italic><jats:sub>max</jats:sub> LF estimator capable of simultaneously correcting for spectroscopic, <jats:italic>r</jats:italic>-band magnitude, and emission-line incompleteness. We studied the contribution to the LF of different types of ELGs classified using two methods: (i) the value of the specific star formation rate (sSFR), and (ii) the line ratios on the Baldwin–Phillips–Terlevich (BPT) and the WHAN (i.e., H<jats:italic>α</jats:italic> equivalent width, EW<jats:sub>H<jats:italic>α</jats:italic></jats:sub>, versus the [N <jats:sc>II</jats:sc>]/H<jats:italic>α</jats:italic> line ratio) diagrams.</jats:p>
<jats:p><jats:italic>Results.</jats:italic> The ELGs in our sample are mostly star forming, with 84 percent having sSFR > 10<jats:sup>−11</jats:sup> yr<jats:sup>−1</jats:sup>. When classifying ELGs using the BPT+WHAN diagrams, we find that 63.3 percent are star forming, only 0.03 are passively evolving, and 1.3 have nuclear activity (Seyfert). The rest are low-ionization narrow emission-line regions (LINERs) and composite ELGs. We found that a Saunders function is the most appropriate to describe all of the emission-line LFs, both observed and dust-extinction-corrected (i.e., intrinsic). They are dominated by star-forming regions, except for the bright end of the [O <jats:sc>III</jats:sc>] and [N <jats:sc>II</jats:sc>] LFs (i.e., <jats:italic>L</jats:italic><jats:sub>[N <jats:sc>II</jats:sc>]</jats:sub> > 10<jats:sup>42</jats:sup> erg s<jats:sup>−1</jats:sup>, <jats:italic>L</jats:italic><jats:sub>[O <jats:sc>III</jats:sc>]</jats:sub> > 10<jats:sup>43</jats:sup> erg s<jats:sup>−1</jats:sup>), where the contribution of Seyfert galaxies is not negligible. In addition to the star-forming population, composite galaxies, and LINERs are the ones that contribute the most to the ELG numbers at <jats:italic>L</jats:italic> < 10<jats:sup>41</jats:sup> erg s<jats:sup>−1</jats:sup>. We do not observe significant evolution with redshift of our ELGs at 0.02 < <jats:italic>z</jats:italic> < 0.22. All of our results, including data points and analytical fits, are publicly available.</jats:p>
<jats:p><jats:italic>Conclusions.</jats:italic> Local ELGs are dominated by star-forming galaxies, except for the brightest [N <jats:sc>II</jats:sc>] and [O <jats:sc>III</jats:sc>] emitters, which have a large contribution of Seyfert galaxies. The local line luminosity functions are best described by Saunders functions. We expect these two conclusions to hold up at higher redshifts for the ELG targeted by current cosmological surveys, such as DESI and <jats:italic>Euclid</jats:italic>.</jats:p>
title: Characterizing the ELG luminosity functions in the nearby Universe

Falcón-Barroso J., & Knapen J. H. 2013, Secular Evolution of Galaxies (Cambridge, UK: Cambridge University Press)
LSST Dark Energy Science Collaboration 2012, arXiv e-prints [arXiv:1211.0310]
LSST Science Collaboration (Abell P. A., et al.) 2009, arXiv e-prints [arXiv:0912.0201]
Osterbrock D. E., & Ferland G. J. 1989, Astrophysics of Gaseous Nebulae and Active Galactic Nuclei (Sausalito, CA: University Science Books)
